old_claim.note: Charterparty Shipping Contracts provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Long-distance maritime trade routes created the repeated voyage risks and capital-allocation problem addressed by sea loans, commenda, and early insurance-style contracts.

source_shape.source_locator: Introduction says medieval insurance contracts protected short- and long-distance maritime trading activities from the North Sea to the Middle East, and that maritime trade risks created demand for protection.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source ties medieval insurance-style risk pooling to long-distance maritime trading routes and their voyage risks.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Trade routes are the direct risk-producing commercial setting for the scoped sea-loan, commenda, and insurance-style contracts; charterparty hiring contracts are a separate shipping-document form.

ontology_before: Merchant Risk Pooling depended on charterparty shipping contracts, implying ship-hire contracts were the direct mechanism for risk pooling.
ontology_after: Sea Loan & Commenda Risk Sharing depends on the long-distance maritime trade setting that created voyage risks and capital-allocation needs.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: merchant_risk_pooling now depends on trade_routes and no longer directly depends on charterparty_shipping_contracts.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to charterparty ship-hire agreements rather than maritime risk-sharing finance.
A stronger source shows charterparty shipping contracts were the direct precursor to medieval sea loans or commenda risk sharing.
A separate charterparty-risk-allocation node is added and should take the charterparty dependency.
short_reason: The source-backed setting is long-distance maritime trade risk, not charterparty ship hire.
